messages: I'm preparing for an on-site interview with Aurelius Analytics for a Senior Machine Learning Engineer position on April 14, 2026 at 10:30 AM. The panel will include Dr. Elena Morris and Samir Patel.

They sent an agenda that lists a 90-minute coding exercise followed by a 12-minute slot for a 10-slide presentation. They also attached a take-home assignment called 'scaling-order-service' that must be submitted by midnight on April 12.

The recruiter said I should bring a government-issued photo ID (passport or driver's license) and mentioned the requisition number REQ-1147. They also asked me to state my salary target when prompted; I'm aiming for $125,000.

My commute is about 42 minutes door-to-door using subway line 2 and then bus 48, so I need to time things carefully. My usual pre-departure routine—charging devices, a quick run-through, packing snacks—takes 20 minutes. Also, I can't rehearse on weekends because of childcare, so practice is limited to weeknights after 7 PM.
Also, I prefer to use my own laptop for code samples, but the recruiter said they'll provide whiteboards. They asked for a printed one-page résumé and a USB stick with my slides.
Should I script my answer to 'Tell me about yourself' word-for-word, or is it better to have bullet points and speak more naturally?
If I only have about a minute to introduce myself to a technical audience, what three things should I try to cover?
I get jittery right before interviews—any quick breathing or grounding practices that work reliably in the five minutes beforehand?
What's a neutral, professional outfit that tends to work well for interviews with both engineers and product people?
How do I convey enthusiasm for a role without sounding like I'll drop everything for it?
What's a compact way to explain a hard technical trade-off, like latency versus consistency, to a mixed audience?
I don't have someone to do mock interviews with—what solo exercises can simulate the pressure and pacing?
Is timing my answers with a stopwatch useful practice, or does it make me sound rehearsed?
Can you suggest five thoughtful questions to ask at the end that demonstrate strategic thinking?
Would mentioning a hobby like cooking or climbing help show character, or can it come across as irrelevant?
What's the most appropriate format for a thank-you note: very brief or a paragraph that recaps highlights?
Could you give a one-paragraph STAR-style answer to 'Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate'?
If earlier projects didn't have metrics, how can I still frame achievements quantitatively or convincingly?
When an interviewer pauses after asking a question, should I fill the silence or take a breath to think?
Do you have a short mental mantra or phrase I can repeat quietly to boost confidence before entering the room?
Recommend three instrumental tracks that calm focus without making me overly amped.
What are discreet breakfast options that provide steady energy but don't cause bad breath?
How deep should I go into security or privacy considerations when briefly describing a past project?
If I only have 30 seconds to describe a side project, what's a tight template to show impact?
Is it okay to hand a concise one-page project summary to an interested interviewer, or is that odd?
What's a polite, concise script to decline an offer by email if I choose another opportunity?
How can I tell, quickly and politely, whether a light joke landed well in a professional conversation?
What common phrasing in answers tends to raise red flags about someone's teamwork or adaptability?
Should I ask about career progression during an early technical interview, or save that for a later conversation?
How do I phrase leadership experience on my resume if I led initiatives but never had direct reports?
If I blank on a small detail from my résumé during a panel question, what's a graceful recovery line?
When do employers typically check references—before an offer is made or after?
Do you know any entertaining or famous interview anecdotes I could read to lighten my mood?
If I want to arrive 15 minutes early to the appointment, and I use the commute estimate I shared earlier, what time should I plan to leave my front door?

They only allocated a short slot for my slide deck; given the slide count I mentioned earlier, if I rehearse evenly and reserve a 90-second buffer at the end, how many seconds per slide should I target?
The take-home is due a couple of days before the meeting—if I realistically have three weeknights with about three hours each to work on it, is that likely to be enough time?
One member of the panel has an academic title while the other does not—how should I address both politely and concisely when greeting them?
For the timed technical portion they scheduled, if I want to save the final 10% for review, how would you break down the remaining time between planning, implementing, and testing?
When negotiation time comes, what’s the best way to present the compensation figure I told you earlier to sound reasonable but still leave room to negotiate?

Finally, taking into account my usual pre-departure routine length that I shared earlier and the commute estimate, at what time should I set a single alarm to start getting ready so I can leave with the early arrival buffer I want?
